meta.title: DBX-DAA-3: Advanced SQL, Visualization and Governance

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to access Databricks SQL to run queries against the company's Delta tables. Which of the following is a prerequisite for the analyst to run queries in Databricks SQL?

options.text: The analyst must have a running all-purpose cluster attached to their notebook
The analyst must have CAN_USE permission on at least one running or startable SQL Warehouse
The analyst must be a workspace administrator to access the SQL persona
The analyst must install the Databricks SQL driver on their local machine

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because to run queries in Databricks SQL, the analyst needs CAN_USE or higher permission on a SQL Warehouse — this is the compute resource that powers query execution. Without access to a warehouse, queries cannot be submitted.

**A** is wrong because all-purpose clusters are used for notebook-based development and are entirely separate from Databricks SQL warehouses.

**C** is wrong because workspace administrator privileges are not required to use Databricks SQL — it is accessible to any user with appropriate permissions on a warehouse and the relevant data.

**D** is wrong because the Databricks SQL interface is web-based and accessed through the browser; no local driver installation is required for the browser UI.

[Databricks SQL overview](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A company runs financial reporting queries every day at 6 AM on a Classic SQL Warehouse with auto-stop set to 15 minutes. The queries take 10 minutes total. The CFO complains that the first query each morning takes much longer than subsequent queries. What explains this behavior, and what change would reduce the morning delay?

options.text: The first query runs full scans while later queries benefit from query result caching; disabling auto-stop would not help
The Classic Warehouse must start up its virtual machine instances each morning after auto-stopping; switching to a Serverless Warehouse reduces cold-start time significantly
The first query loads table metadata while subsequent queries use cached metadata; clearing the metadata cache resets this
Classic warehouses throttle the first query of each day to prioritize Databricks jobs; this cannot be changed

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a Classic SQL Warehouse stops its underlying cloud virtual machine instances when auto-stop triggers. The next morning, the first query must wait for VM provisioning and cluster initialization (cold start), which can take 1–5 minutes. Serverless SQL Warehouses use pre-provisioned compute infrastructure managed by Databricks, resulting in significantly faster start times (typically seconds rather than minutes).

**A** is wrong because while the query result cache does accelerate repeated identical queries, the cold-start delay is due to warehouse startup, not cache state — and disabling auto-stop would keep the warehouse warm, eliminating the delay.

**C** is wrong because metadata loading is a minor factor and does not account for the multi-minute morning delay.

**D** is wrong because Databricks does not throttle first-of-day queries; the delay is an infrastructure start-up issue.

[Create a SQL warehouse](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/admin/create-sql-warehouse.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst saves a query and runs it. The results are displayed in 3 seconds from the result cache. A system administrator then runs `REFRESH TABLE sales.transactions` on the underlying table. A different analyst submits the exact same query 2 minutes later. What happens?

options.text: The second analyst gets results in 3 seconds from the cache, because REFRESH TABLE does not affect the query result cache
The second analyst receives an error because the cache is locked while the table is being refreshed
The second analyst gets results in 3 seconds, but the results reflect the refreshed metadata only — not new data files
The second analyst waits for the full query execution because REFRESH TABLE invalidates the query result cache for that table

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because REFRESH TABLE in Databricks SQL forces the metastore to re-sync its metadata for the table (refreshing cached file lists, statistics, and schema information). This operation invalidates the query result cache for the affected table, causing subsequent queries to re-execute against the fresh table state rather than returning the cached result.

**A** is wrong because REFRESH TABLE does invalidate the query result cache — it signals that the table's data or metadata has changed.

**C** is wrong because when the result cache is invalidated, the query fully re-executes against the actual data files, not just refreshed metadata.

**B** is wrong because no lock error occurs — the cache invalidation is transparent and the query simply re-executes.

[REFRESH TABLE syntax](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-syntax-aux-cache-refresh-table.html)

questions.stem: A data team wants to set up an alert that sends a message to a Slack channel whenever daily error rate exceeds 5%. The team lead creates a Databricks SQL Alert. Which of the following correctly describes how to configure the Slack notification?

options.text: Enter the Slack channel name (e.g., #alerts) in the notification destination field of the alert
Install the Databricks Slack App from the Slack marketplace, which automatically links alerts to channels
Use a Databricks Workflow to call the Slack API whenever the alert query result exceeds the threshold
Configure a Slack webhook URL as a notification destination in Databricks SQL, then select it when creating the alert

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Databricks SQL Alerts integrate with Slack through webhook URLs. The team must first create an Incoming Webhook in the Slack workspace and obtain the webhook URL, then add it as a notification destination in Databricks SQL (under SQL > Settings > Notification Destinations or within the alert configuration). When the alert triggers, Databricks posts a message to the webhook URL, which Slack routes to the target channel.

**A** is wrong because channel names alone are not sufficient — a webhook URL that authenticates with the specific Slack workspace and channel is required.

**C** is wrong because while a Databricks Workflow could achieve this, it is not the purpose-built mechanism — Databricks SQL Alerts with webhook destinations are the correct native feature.

**B** is wrong because there is no official Databricks Slack marketplace app that automatically links DBSQL alerts to channels; webhook configuration is the supported integration method.

[Databricks SQL alerts](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/alerts/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to find all queries submitted in the last 30 days that took longer than 5 minutes to run, to identify performance bottlenecks. In Databricks SQL, where is this information available?

options.text: In the SQL Editor's recent queries tab, which stores queries from the current browser session
In the Databricks Workspace admin console under Audit Logs
In the Query History page, which can be filtered by date range and sorted by duration
In the SQL Warehouse event log, which records all queries and their runtimes

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the Query History page in Databricks SQL is specifically designed to show all queries run on the workspace's SQL Warehouses, with the ability to filter by user, warehouse, date range, query status, and sort by duration to find the slowest queries.

**A** is wrong because the SQL Editor's recent queries tab is limited to the current user's own queries in the current session.

**B** is wrong because Audit Logs are workspace-level logs for security and compliance, not a performance analysis tool within Databricks SQL.

**D** is wrong because the warehouse event log tracks cluster start/stop events, not individual query execution and runtime details.

[Query history](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/admin/query-history.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a SQL Warehouse for the analytics team. Select TWO TRUE statements about the relationship between SQL Warehouse permissions and data access permissions in Databricks. (Select TWO)

options.text: Having CAN_USE permission on a SQL Warehouse does not automatically grant access to any tables or schemas
A user with CAN_USE on a warehouse can query any table in the metastore because the warehouse provides compute access
Table-level SELECT grants are needed separately, in addition to warehouse access, for a user to query specific tables
CAN_MANAGE on a warehouse also grants SELECT access to all tables the warehouse can reach

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because SQL Warehouse permissions (CAN_USE, CAN_MANAGE) control access to the compute resource itself — they do not automatically grant access to any data. Warehouse access and data access are governed by separate permission systems.

**C** is correct because a user needs both: (1) CAN_USE on the warehouse to execute queries, and (2) appropriate table-level or schema-level privileges (SELECT, or ALL PRIVILEGES) to read the data. Both are required independently.

**B** is wrong because compute access (warehouse) and data access (tables) are separate concerns — CAN_USE on a warehouse does not bypass table-level permissions.

**D** is wrong because CAN_MANAGE is a warehouse administrative permission that controls warehouse configuration and permissions management, not data access — it grants no additional data privileges.

[Unity Catalog overview](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst runs a complex query that aggregates 500 million rows and returns 10 result rows in 45 seconds. The exact same query is submitted again 5 minutes later. The data has not changed. What will the second query's response time be, and why?

options.text: 45 seconds, because the warehouse must re-execute the query each time to ensure accuracy
About 22 seconds, because the result cache stores the raw data for partial reuse
Under 1 second, because Photon's disk cache has warmed up the data files for faster scanning
Under 1 second, because the query result cache returns the cached 10-row result without re-executing the query

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Databricks SQL caches the exact output of a query (the 10-row result set) for up to 24 hours. When the identical query is submitted again within the cache validity period and the underlying table has not changed, the cached result is returned immediately — no data scanning or computation occurs.

**A** is wrong because the result cache specifically avoids re-executing identical queries against unchanged data.

**C** is wrong because while the disk cache (I/O cache) warms up file reads, it still requires query execution to scan the files — the result cache skips execution entirely by caching the final output.

**B** is wrong because the result cache returns the exact complete result set, not raw data for partial reuse — there is no 'partial reuse' mechanism that produces a 50% speedup.

[SQL Warehouse and result caching](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/admin/create-sql-warehouse.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to query data from a Delta table as it existed 7 days ago using Time Travel. The `orders` table has been updated daily since then. Which query correctly reads the table as of 7 days ago?

options.text: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 7 DAYS
SELECT * FROM orders AT (TIMESTAMP => NOW() - INTERVAL 7 DAYS)
SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 7
SELECT * FROM orders TIMESTAMP AS OF DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, 7)

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because the Delta Lake Time Travel syntax `TIMESTAMP AS OF <timestamp>` reads the table as it existed at the specified point in time. DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, 7) returns the date 7 days ago, so this query returns the table state from 7 days ago.

**A** is wrong because this query filters rows by their order_date column value — it does not use Delta Time Travel to retrieve a historical table state.

**C** is wrong because `VERSION AS OF 7` reads the table at Delta version number 7, which may not correspond to 7 days ago — version numbers are sequential integers based on commits, not days.

**B** is wrong because `AT (TIMESTAMP =>` is not valid Spark SQL Time Travel syntax; the correct syntax is `TIMESTAMP AS OF`.

[Delta Lake time travel](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/history.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a view in Databricks SQL:

```sql
CREATE VIEW analytics.active_customers AS
SELECT customer_id, name, email
FROM raw.customers
WHERE status = 'active';
```

A colleague is granted SELECT on `analytics.active_customers`. Can the colleague access the `raw.customers` table directly?

options.text: Yes, because the view is built on raw.customers, so access to the view implies access to the base table
Yes, but only for the columns included in the view (customer_id, name, email)
No, granting SELECT on the view does not grant SELECT on the underlying raw.customers base table
No, the colleague cannot even use the view because they need raw.customers access first

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because in Databricks SQL, granting SELECT on a view is independent of the base table permissions. The colleague can query the view and receive its results without having any explicit permission on `raw.customers` — the view executes with the view owner's permissions or the permissions of the user running the query, depending on the access control model. This is a key security benefit of views.

**A** is wrong because access to a view does NOT implicitly grant access to the underlying tables — they are separate permission grants.

**B** is wrong because the colleague cannot access raw.customers at all, not even for the view's columns — they can only query the view.

**D** is wrong because the colleague CAN use the view without needing direct access to the base table, which is exactly what makes views useful for access control.

[Databricks SQL views and access control](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst runs `OPTIMIZE sales.orders ZORDER BY (customer_id, order_date);`. What does the ZORDER BY clause accomplish beyond the standard OPTIMIZE file compaction?

options.text: It sorts the data within each file by customer_id and order_date so that queries filtering by these columns can skip irrelevant files using data skipping
It creates a traditional B-tree index on customer_id and order_date for fast lookups
It partitions the table by customer_id and order_date, creating separate directories for each value combination
It materializes query results for all queries that filter on customer_id and order_date into a cache

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Z-Ordering is a multi-dimensional clustering technique that co-locates related values of the specified columns within the same data files. Delta Lake stores min/max statistics for each file, and Z-Ordering ensures that queries filtering on customer_id or order_date can use file-level data skipping to skip entire files whose min/max range does not contain the queried value. This dramatically reduces the amount of data scanned for selective queries.

**B** is wrong because Delta Lake does not use traditional B-tree indexes — Z-Ordering uses file-level statistics and data skipping, not an index structure.

**C** is wrong because Z-Ordering is not partitioning — it reorganizes data within the existing file layout without creating directory-based partitions.

**D** is wrong because Z-Ordering organizes physical data layout, not query result caching.

[Delta Lake OPTIMIZE and Z-ORDER](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/optimizations/file-mgmt.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to create a new table in the `reporting` schema and grant a team of analysts read-only access to all tables in that schema. Select TWO SQL statements that together accomplish these tasks correctly. (Select TWO)

options.text: CREATE TABLE reporting.monthly_kpis USING DELTA AS SELECT * FROM staging.kpi_data;
CREATE TABLE reporting.monthly_kpis AS SELECT * FROM staging.kpi_data STORED AS PARQUET;
GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA reporting TO `analysts_group`;
GRANT CAN_VIEW ON SCHEMA reporting TO `analysts_group`;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because CREATE TABLE ... USING DELTA AS SELECT ... is the valid Databricks SQL CTAS (Create Table As Select) syntax that creates a Delta table from a query result.

**B** is wrong because STORED AS PARQUET is Hive syntax and is not valid in Databricks SQL — the correct syntax uses USING DELTA (or USING PARQUET for Parquet format, but STORED AS is not the keyword).

**C** is correct because GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA grants SELECT privilege on all current and future tables in the reporting schema to the analysts_group, achieving the read-only access goal.

**D** is wrong because CAN_VIEW is a dashboard/query permission level in the Databricks SQL UI — it is not a SQL schema-level privilege. The correct SQL privilege for read access is SELECT.

[Databricks SQL DDL and grants](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to create a materialized view in Databricks SQL to store pre-aggregated results for a frequently-run expensive query. Which statement correctly creates a materialized view?

options.text: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW reporting.daily_sales AS SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY order_date;
CREATE VIEW MATERIALIZED reporting.daily_sales AS SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY order_date;
CREATE TABLE reporting.daily_sales AS SELECT order_date, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY order_date;
Materialized views are not supported in Databricks SQL; pre-aggregated results must be stored as Delta tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW is the valid Databricks SQL syntax for creating a materialized view, which stores the pre-computed result of the query and can be automatically refreshed. Materialized views in Databricks SQL are supported as a first-class object that combines the convenience of a view with the performance of materialized results.

**B** is wrong because the keyword order CREATE VIEW MATERIALIZED is incorrect syntax — the correct syntax places MATERIALIZED before VIEW.

**C** is wrong because while CREATE TABLE AS SELECT creates a static snapshot of data, it does not behave like a materialized view — it cannot be automatically refreshed and is not tracked as a view object.

**D** is wrong because materialized views are indeed supported in Databricks SQL.

[Materialized views in Databricks SQL](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following query to calculate each salesperson's revenue as a percentage of their team's total revenue:

```sql
SELECT
  team_id,
  salesperson_name,
  revenue,
  revenue / SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY team_id) * 100 AS pct_of_team
FROM sales_performance;
```

What does `SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY team_id)` calculate in this context?

options.text: The sum of revenue for each individual salesperson row
The running total of revenue from the first row to the current row within each team
The total revenue for each team, replicated in every row for that team
The grand total revenue across all teams

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because a window function with PARTITION BY team_id and no ORDER BY or ROWS/RANGE frame specification defaults to the entire partition as the window. SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY team_id) calculates the total revenue for all rows in the same team and places that total value in every row of that team's partition — enabling the division to calculate each salesperson's share.

**A** is wrong because the window function operates across all rows in the partition, not just the current row's value.

**B** is wrong because a running total would require an ORDER BY clause inside the OVER clause and a ROWS BETWEEN frame — without ORDER BY, the window spans the entire partition.

**D** is wrong because PARTITION BY team_id scopes the sum to each team independently, not across all teams (which would require OVER() with no PARTITION BY).

[Databricks SQL window functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst runs the following query:

```sql
SELECT
  region,
  product_category,
  SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
FROM sales
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (region, product_category),
  (region),
  ()
);
```

How many rows would this query return if there are 4 regions and 5 product categories, with data in every combination?

options.text: 20 rows (4 regions × 5 categories)
24 rows (20 detail + 4 region subtotals)
25 rows (20 detail + 4 region subtotals + 1 grand total)
29 rows (20 detail + 4 region subtotals + 5 category subtotals + 1 grand total — same as CUBE)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because GROUPING SETS explicitly lists which groupings to compute. This query has three grouping sets: (region, product_category) produces 4×5=20 rows; (region) produces 4 rows (one per region); () — the empty set — produces 1 grand total row. Total: 20 + 4 + 1 = 25 rows.

**A** is wrong because it only counts the detail rows and misses the subtotals and grand total.

**B** is wrong because 24 rows omits the grand total row generated by the empty grouping set ().

**D** is wrong because the query uses GROUPING SETS, not CUBE — it does not include the 5 product_category-only subtotal rows. CUBE would produce all combinations including (product_category) and () which gives 20 + 4 + 5 + 1 = 30 rows.

[GROUPING SETS in Databricks SQL](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to combine the results of two queries into one result set, including ALL rows from both queries (including duplicates). Which SQL set operation should be used?

options.text: UNION
UNION ALL
INTERSECT
EXCEPT

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because UNION ALL returns all rows from both queries including exact duplicates. It does not perform deduplication, making it faster than UNION.

**A** is wrong because UNION returns the combined result set but removes duplicate rows — it implicitly performs a DISTINCT operation. UNION ALL preserves duplicates and is the correct choice when all rows including duplicates are needed.

**C** is wrong because INTERSECT returns only rows that appear in BOTH result sets — rows that exist in one but not the other are excluded.

**D** is wrong because EXCEPT (also written as MINUS) returns rows from the first query that do NOT appear in the second query.

[SQL set operations](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to find products that appear in both the `online_sales` table and the `store_sales` table (matching on `product_id`). Select TWO queries that correctly return only the products that exist in BOTH tables. (Select TWO)

options.text: SELECT product_id FROM online_sales INTERSECT SELECT product_id FROM store_sales
SELECT DISTINCT o.product_id FROM online_sales o INNER JOIN store_sales s ON o.product_id = s.product_id
SELECT product_id FROM online_sales UNION SELECT product_id FROM store_sales
SELECT product_id FROM online_sales WHERE product_id NOT IN (SELECT product_id FROM store_sales)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because INTERSECT is the set operation that returns exactly the rows present in both result sets — in this case, product IDs that appear in both online_sales and store_sales.

**B** is correct because an INNER JOIN returns only matching rows from both tables; adding DISTINCT ensures each product_id appears only once even if a product has multiple sales records in either table.

**C** is wrong because UNION (without ALL) returns all unique product IDs from either table — it does not require a product to exist in both.

**D** is wrong because NOT IN returns products from online_sales that are NOT in store_sales — the exact opposite of what is needed.

[SQL set operations and joins](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to extract email domain names from an `email_address` column. For example, 'john.doe@company.com' should return 'company.com'. Which expression correctly extracts the domain?

options.text: SUBSTRING(email_address, INSTR(email_address, '@') + 1)
SPLIT(email_address, '@')[1]
REGEXP_EXTRACT(email_address, '@(.+)$', 1)
SUBSTR(email_address, CHARINDEX('@', email_address) + 1)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `SUBSTRING(string, position)` returns characters starting from the given position through the end of the string. `INSTR(email_address, '@')` returns the position of the '@' character, so adding 1 gives the position immediately after '@'. For 'john.doe@company.com', INSTR returns 9, so SUBSTRING starts at position 10, returning 'company.com'.

**B** is a plausible alternative — `SPLIT(email_address, '@')[1]` would also produce 'company.com' in Spark SQL, but SPLIT returns an array and array indexing syntax may vary by context. SUBSTRING with INSTR is the more universally portable string function approach.

**C** is a plausible alternative — `REGEXP_EXTRACT(email_address, '@(.+)$', 1)` captures everything after '@' using a regex group, which also returns 'company.com'. However, it is overkill for a simple email domain extraction when string functions suffice.

**D** is wrong because `CHARINDEX` is a T-SQL (Microsoft SQL Server) function, not a valid Spark SQL function. The equivalent Spark SQL function is `INSTR`, not `CHARINDEX`.

[Built-in string functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-functions-builtin.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes this CTE query:

```sql
WITH customer_orders AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(revenue) AS lifetime_value
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
high_value AS (
  SELECT customer_id
  FROM customer_orders
  WHERE lifetime_value > 10000
)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.name, co.total_orders, co.lifetime_value
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN customer_orders co ON c.customer_id = co.customer_id
INNER JOIN high_value hv ON c.customer_id = hv.customer_id;
```

What does this query return?

options.text: All customers with their order count and lifetime value
An error because a CTE cannot reference another CTE
All orders grouped by customer with a running total of revenue
Customers with a lifetime value over $10,000, along with their order count and lifetime value

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because the `customer_orders` CTE aggregates all orders by customer, and the `high_value` CTE filters to only customers with lifetime_value > 10000. The final query joins customers to customer_orders (for the metrics) and inner joins to high_value (which restricts results to high-value customers only). The result is customer name, total orders, and lifetime value for customers who have spent more than $10,000.

**A** is wrong because the INNER JOIN to high_value filters out customers below the $10,000 threshold.

**C** is wrong because the CTE performs grouped aggregation, not a running total — and the result includes customer attributes, not individual order rows.

**B** is wrong because CTEs can absolutely reference previously defined CTEs in the same WITH clause — this is standard and valid Spark SQL.

[Common Table Expressions in Databricks SQL](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to convert an order timestamp to a formatted string for display in a report. The `order_ts` column is TIMESTAMP and the required output format is 'MM/DD/YYYY HH:mm'. Which function produces this formatted string?

options.text: TO_DATE(order_ts, 'MM/DD/YYYY HH:mm')
DATE_FORMAT(order_ts, 'MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm')
CAST(order_ts AS STRING)
FORMAT_DATE(order_ts, 'MM/DD/YYYY HH:mm')

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because DATE_FORMAT(timestamp, pattern) converts a DATE or TIMESTAMP value to a formatted string using Java SimpleDateFormat patterns. The pattern 'MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm' produces the desired output with two-digit month (MM), two-digit day (dd), four-digit year (yyyy), and 24-hour hours (HH) with minutes. Note that the case matters: 'MM' for month, 'dd' for day, 'yyyy' for year, 'HH' for 24-hour hour.

**A** is wrong because TO_DATE converts a string to a DATE type using a parse pattern — it does not format a timestamp as a string.

**C** is wrong because CAST(order_ts AS STRING) produces a default timestamp string representation (e.g., '2024-03-15 09:30:00') without the specified format.

**D** is wrong because FORMAT_DATE is not a valid Spark SQL function name — the correct function is DATE_FORMAT.

[DATE_FORMAT and timestamp functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes a correlated subquery:

```sql
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.name,
  (SELECT MAX(order_date)
   FROM orders o
   WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) AS last_order_date
FROM customers c;
```

Which statement correctly describes what makes this a correlated subquery, and what a potential performance concern is?

options.text: The subquery is correlated because it references the outer query's alias c.customer_id; for large customer tables, the subquery may execute once per customer row
The subquery is not correlated — it runs once and its results are joined to customers
The subquery is correlated but Spark SQL always rewrites it as a JOIN automatically with no performance impact
The subquery is correlated because it uses MAX(), which requires a full scan of the orders table for every query

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a correlated subquery references columns from the outer query (here, c.customer_id from the outer customers alias). This creates a dependency where the subquery logically runs once per row of the outer query, substituting the current customer's ID into the WHERE clause. For millions of customers, this can be performance-intensive.

**B** is wrong because the subquery is correlated — it references the outer table alias c, not an independent subquery.

**C** is wrong because while Spark SQL's optimizer does attempt to rewrite correlated subqueries as joins (decorrelation), it is not guaranteed for all patterns and complex correlated subqueries may still be executed row-by-row.

**D** is wrong because the correlation is defined by the reference to the outer query alias (c.customer_id), not by the use of MAX().

[Correlated subqueries in Spark SQL](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to format a product description by trimming whitespace and converting it to title case. The column `raw_description` contains values like '  wireless keyboard  '. Which combination of functions produces 'Wireless Keyboard'?

options.text: UPPER(TRIM(raw_description))
LOWER(TRIM(raw_description))
INITCAP(TRIM(raw_description))
TRIM(TITLE(raw_description))

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because INITCAP() converts the first letter of each word to uppercase and the remaining letters to lowercase (title case), and TRIM() removes leading and trailing whitespace. Applying TRIM first removes the spaces, then INITCAP converts 'wireless keyboard' to 'Wireless Keyboard'.

**A** is wrong because UPPER() converts the entire string to uppercase, producing 'WIRELESS KEYBOARD', not title case.

**B** is wrong because LOWER() converts the entire string to lowercase, producing 'wireless keyboard' — no capitalization.

**D** is wrong because TITLE() is not a valid Spark SQL function; the correct function for title case is INITCAP().

[Built-in string functions — INITCAP and TRIM](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst is asked NOT to use a pie chart to represent a dataset. In which scenario is a pie chart considered INAPPROPRIATE?

options.text: Showing the percentage share of 4 product categories in annual revenue (categories total 100%)
Showing the budget allocation across 3 departments
Comparing the proportion of customer segments (Enterprise, Mid-Market, SMB) in the customer base
Showing the revenue trend across 24 months

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because pie charts show composition at a single point in time — they are fundamentally unsuitable for showing change over time (trends). A time series with 24 data points belongs on a line chart.

**A** is wrong because 4 categories totaling 100% is a classic appropriate use case for a pie chart — few slices, clear proportional comparison.

**C** is wrong because comparing proportions of 3 customer segments is an appropriate pie chart use case.

**B** is wrong because budget allocation across 3 departments is a clear proportional composition scenario appropriate for a pie chart.

[Databricks SQL dashboards and visualizations](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a Databricks SQL dashboard with a dropdown parameter listing sales regions. The parameter is of type 'Query-based Dropdown'. The underlying lookup query is:

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT region FROM regions ORDER BY region
```

A new region is added to the `regions` table. When will the new region appear in the dropdown?

options.text: Immediately, because query-based dropdowns refresh every 30 seconds in real time
Never, because the dropdown list is static and was captured when the parameter was created
The next time the dashboard is loaded or the parameter query is refreshed
After 24 hours, when the query result cache for the lookup query expires

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because query-based dropdown parameters in Databricks SQL re-execute the lookup query to populate their values when the dashboard is loaded or the parameter is refreshed. The dropdown list is not permanently cached — it is refreshed from the source query, so new regions added to the regions table will appear in the dropdown the next time the page loads or the parameter is refreshed.

**A** is wrong because dropdowns do not auto-refresh every 30 seconds — they refresh on page load or manual refresh.

**B** is wrong because query-based dropdowns are dynamic and re-execute the source query, not static lists frozen at creation time.

**D** is wrong because the 24-hour cache applies to result sets from data queries, not necessarily parameter dropdown queries, and reloading the dashboard typically triggers a fresh lookup query execution.

[Dashboard query-based parameters](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst builds a dashboard that shows daily website sessions. The analyst adds a bar chart showing sessions by day and a line chart showing a 7-day rolling average — both driven by the same query. The analyst wants users to be able to filter both charts to a specific date range using a single filter widget. What is the most efficient way to configure this?

options.text: Create two separate queries (one per chart) and add two separate date range parameters named differently
Use a single query with a date range parameter, and configure both chart visualizations from that single query
Use the same parameter name in both queries so both charts update when the shared parameter changes
Both B and C are valid approaches; B uses one query for both charts while C uses separate queries with a shared parameter name

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because there are two valid approaches. option **B** (one query, two visualizations) is the most efficient — a single query with a parameter can power multiple chart visualizations on the same dashboard; both charts reference the same query result and both update when the parameter changes. option **C** (two queries, same parameter name) also works — when two queries use a parameter with the same name, a single dashboard parameter widget controls both. Both approaches are valid, making D the most complete answer.

**A** is wrong because two differently-named parameters would require two separate filter widgets, which is less user-friendly and would not synchronize automatically.

[Dashboard parameters and shared queries](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst creates an alert to monitor inventory levels. The alert query returns the count of items below minimum stock level. The analyst wants to be notified when stock falls below the threshold AND wants a notification when stock recovers above the threshold. Select TWO TRUE statements about configuring this in Databricks SQL Alerts. (Select TWO)

options.text: The alert can be configured to send a notification when the condition transitions from OK to TRIGGERED (stock falls below threshold)
Databricks SQL Alerts can be configured to send a recovery notification when the alert returns to OK status
Two separate alerts must be created: one for 'below threshold' and one for 'above threshold'
Alerts only send notifications when triggered (condition met) and never notify when recovering to OK

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because alerts are designed to notify when the condition is first met (transitions from OK to TRIGGERED).

**B** is correct because Databricks SQL Alerts have an option to send a recovery notification when the alert condition returns to OK (the value recovers above the threshold), which can be configured in the alert notification settings.

**C** is wrong because a single alert manages both states (TRIGGERED and OK/recovered) — two alerts are not needed to cover both scenarios.

**D** is wrong because Databricks SQL Alerts DO support recovery notifications when the state transitions from TRIGGERED back to OK, which is exactly the feature the analyst needs.

[Databricks SQL alert states and notifications](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/alerts/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to add interactivity to a dashboard so that viewers can narrow down a table of sales records by typing in a customer name. Which Databricks SQL parameter type is most appropriate for a free-text customer name search?

options.text: Date parameter
Number parameter
Text parameter
Query-based dropdown parameter

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because a Text parameter creates a free-text input field on the dashboard where users can type any string value — ideal for searching by a customer name where the possible values are too numerous to enumerate in a dropdown. The parameter value can then be used in a LIKE or ILIKE filter in the SQL query.

**A** is wrong because a Date parameter creates a date picker widget and only accepts date values, not text strings.

**B** is wrong because a Number parameter accepts only numeric input, which is inappropriate for a customer name search.

**D** is wrong because a query-based dropdown shows a discrete list of selectable values from a lookup query — for an open-ended name search, a text input is more appropriate than a dropdown of potentially thousands of customer names.

[Dashboard parameter types](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst builds a dashboard with the following query:

```sql
SELECT product, region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
FROM sales
WHERE order_date >= '{{ start_date }}'
  AND order_date <= '{{ end_date }}'
GROUP BY product, region
```

The dashboard is published with default parameter values of start_date='2024-01-01' and end_date='2024-12-31'. A user with CAN_RUN permission opens the dashboard. What can the user do?

options.text: View the default results only — CAN_RUN does not allow changing parameter values
Share the dashboard with additional users by granting them CAN_RUN permission
Edit the SQL query to add additional filters and run the modified query
Change the start_date and end_date parameter values and click Run to get results for a different date range

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because CAN_RUN permission on a dashboard allows users to interact with parameter widgets (change the values of start_date and end_date), trigger a fresh query execution, and view the results. This is the core capability that CAN_RUN grants.

**A** is wrong because CAN_RUN explicitly includes the ability to change parameter values and re-run queries — that is the primary purpose of CAN_RUN versus CAN_VIEW (which only shows cached results).

**C** is wrong because editing the underlying SQL query requires CAN_EDIT permission — CAN_RUN does not allow modifying the query definition.

**B** is wrong because managing permissions (sharing with others) requires CAN_MANAGE permission.

[Dashboard permissions — CAN_RUN](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem: A business analyst is comparing the quarterly performance of 6 different product lines over 3 years (12 quarters total). The analyst wants to show both the absolute revenue for each product line per quarter AND make it easy to see the total revenue in each quarter. Which chart type best serves both requirements?

options.text: Grouped bar chart (6 bars per quarter, 12 quarter groups)
Stacked bar chart (bars divided into 6 color-coded segments per quarter)
Line chart with 6 separate lines, one per product line
Pie chart with 72 slices (6 products × 12 quarters)

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a stacked bar chart shows the individual contribution of each product line (visible as a colored segment within each bar) AND the total quarterly revenue (the full bar height), simultaneously satisfying both requirements. With 6 products shown as segments, the chart remains readable while conveying both part-to-whole and total comparisons.

**A** is wrong because a grouped bar chart (6 bars per quarter, 72 total bars) makes it easy to compare product lines within a quarter but makes it difficult to see the total for each quarter — viewers must mentally sum 6 bars.

**C** is wrong because a line chart with 6 lines shows trends well but does not visually convey total revenue per quarter.

**D** is wrong because 72-slice pie charts are completely unreadable — pie charts should be limited to a small number of slices.

[Chart visualization types](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst is designing a Databricks SQL dashboard for executives who are not technical users. Select TWO dashboard design best practices that improve usability and performance for non-technical executive audiences. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use counter widgets for the top 3 KPIs (e.g., total revenue, total customers, conversion rate) to give executives an immediate snapshot
Display the underlying SQL queries on the dashboard so executives can validate the data source
Pre-aggregate data in query logic (e.g., GROUP BY month) rather than returning row-level data and relying on the visualization to aggregate
Use a wide SELECT * query to fetch all available columns so executives can choose what to look at in the table widget

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because counter widgets displaying key KPIs are a dashboard design best practice for executive audiences — they provide immediate, scannable information without requiring chart interpretation. Placing the most important metrics as counters at the top of a dashboard follows the F-pattern of visual attention.

**C** is correct because pre-aggregating data in SQL (GROUP BY month) reduces the data volume returned to the dashboard, improves query performance, and produces the level of granularity executives need — avoiding expensive row-level scans for trend charts.

**B** is wrong because displaying raw SQL queries on an executive dashboard adds complexity and confusion for non-technical viewers — queries should be hidden in the underlying data configuration.

**D** is wrong because SELECT * fetches unnecessary data, increases query time, and makes the dashboard slower — only the columns needed for the specific visualizations should be selected.

[Dashboard design best practices](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst creates a Databricks SQL Alert with the condition 'Value is BELOW 0.95' on a query that returns the current data pipeline success rate as a decimal (e.g., 0.98 for 98%). The alert schedule is set to run every 15 minutes. If the success rate drops to 0.90 at 2:00 PM and recovers to 0.97 at 2:30 PM, how many TRIGGERED notifications will be sent in total (assuming no recovery notifications are configured)?

options.text: 1 notification (sent at 2:00 PM when the rate first drops below 0.95)
2 notifications (one at 2:00 PM and one at 2:15 PM when the rate is still below 0.95)
3 notifications (at 2:00 PM, 2:15 PM, and 2:30 PM — one per scheduled run)
0 notifications (the 15-minute schedule means the first evaluation is at 2:15 PM)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Databricks SQL Alerts are state-based — they send a TRIGGERED notification only when the alert transitions from OK state to TRIGGERED state. At 2:00 PM the rate drops to 0.90, crossing the threshold for the first time, and one notification is sent. At 2:15 PM the rate is still 0.90 (still below 0.95), but the alert is already in TRIGGERED state — no additional notification is sent for staying in the triggered state. At 2:30 PM the rate recovers to 0.97, the alert transitions back to OK, but since recovery notifications are not configured, no notification is sent. Total: 1 notification.

**B** is wrong because alerts do not re-send TRIGGERED notifications on every run while the condition remains true — only on the initial OK-to-TRIGGERED transition.

**C** is wrong for the same reason as B.

**D** is wrong because the alert fires at 2:00 PM when the condition is first evaluated as triggered — the schedule determines the evaluation frequency, not a delay before the first evaluation.

[Databricks SQL alert behavior](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/alerts/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst's organization wants to query data from an existing Snowflake data warehouse alongside Delta Lake tables — all within the same Databricks SQL session — without copying any data. Which Databricks feature makes this possible?

options.text: Delta Sharing — publish the Snowflake tables as a Delta Share for Databricks to consume
Lakehouse Federation — register Snowflake as a foreign connection and query it through a Unity Catalog foreign catalog
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE using Snowflake's JDBC URL in the LOCATION clause
COPY INTO — copy Snowflake data into a staging Delta table before querying

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Lakehouse Federation is the Databricks feature that allows registering external data systems (including Snowflake, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redshift, SQL Server, and others) as foreign connections in Unity Catalog. Once registered, the external tables appear as a foreign catalog and can be queried directly from Databricks SQL alongside native Delta tables — no data movement required.

**A** is wrong because Delta Sharing is a data-sharing protocol for Delta Lake data, not a mechanism for federating queries to Snowflake.

**C** is wrong because CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE in Databricks references cloud storage paths (S3, ADLS, GCS), not JDBC connections to external databases.

**D** is wrong because COPY INTO is a data ingestion command that moves data into a Delta table — it copies data rather than federating queries in place.

[Lakehouse Federation and Unity Catalog](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: An administrator is configuring a SQL Warehouse for a finance team and needs to balance performance with cost control. Select TWO TRUE statements about Serverless SQL Warehouse billing compared to Classic SQL Warehouses. (Select TWO)

options.text: Serverless SQL Warehouses bill per second of actual query and startup activity, with faster startup than Classic Warehouses
Classic SQL Warehouses bill for the entire duration the underlying virtual machines are running, including idle time between queries
Serverless SQL Warehouses have higher per-DBU rates than Classic Warehouses for all cluster sizes
Classic SQL Warehouses do not incur any cost during the auto-stop idle period before shutdown

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Serverless SQL Warehouses use per-second billing tied to active compute time. Databricks pre-provisions the infrastructure, so startup time is significantly shorter than Classic Warehouses — reducing the cost of cold starts.

**B** is correct because Classic SQL Warehouses provision cloud VMs that run until the auto-stop threshold is reached. The VMs consume DBUs during all time the warehouse is in the RUNNING state — including idle time between queries.

**A** is wrong in part C's context: while rates differ, the statement in C that serverless is always higher per-DBU is not universally true across all scenarios.

**D** is wrong because Classic Warehouses bill for all time the VMs are running — idle time between queries is still billed at the running rate until auto-stop triggers.

[Serverless vs Classic SQL Warehouse billing](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/admin/create-sql-warehouse.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst runs a query that produces results in 2 seconds due to the query result cache. The analyst's manager wants to see the exact same query re-executed against the live data right now, ignoring the cache. Which approach forces a fresh query execution in Databricks SQL?

options.text: Append a comment to the query such as /* force_refresh */ to make it appear as a different query to the cache
Run INVALIDATE CACHE FOR TABLE sales.orders before re-submitting the query
Change a parameter value or modify the query text so it does not match the cached version, or use REFRESH TABLE to invalidate the cache first
Set spark.databricks.queryResultCache.enabled = false at the session level before running the query

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the query result cache key is based on the exact query text, the warehouse, and the table version. Two approaches work: (1) modify the query text (even a minor change) so it no longer matches the cached query, or (2) run REFRESH TABLE on the underlying table, which invalidates the cache for that table. Either approach forces the system to re-execute the query against live data.

**A** is a partially correct observation — adding a SQL comment does change the query text and would bypass the exact-text cache match, making it technically functional, but it is not the recommended approach.

**B** is wrong because INVALIDATE CACHE is not a standard Databricks SQL command.

**D** is wrong because while session-level configuration may affect some behaviors, there is no standard spark.databricks.queryResultCache.enabled session parameter for disabling the result cache in Databricks SQL.

[Query result cache behavior](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates a deep clone of a production Delta table for an isolated analysis environment:

```sql
CREATE TABLE analytics.orders_clone
DEEP CLONE prod.orders;
```

How does a deep clone differ from a shallow clone, and what happens to the clone if the source table is updated after the clone is created?

options.text: A deep clone copies all data files to a new location; updates to the source table do NOT automatically propagate to the deep clone — it is independent
A deep clone copies all data files; updates to the source table automatically synchronize to the clone because they share a transaction log
A deep clone creates only a metadata copy; data files remain shared with the source, so source updates appear in the clone automatically
A deep clone copies data files and creates a live mirror — it is equivalent to a materialized view that refreshes on a schedule

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a DEEP CLONE copies all data files (Parquet files and the transaction log) to a new physical location. The result is a completely independent copy of the table at the point in time the clone was taken. Subsequent updates to the source table do not affect the clone, and updates to the clone do not affect the source — they maintain entirely separate histories.

**B** is wrong because deep clones are independent copies, not live mirrors — they do not share a transaction log and do not auto-sync.

**C** is wrong because that describes a shallow clone, not a deep clone — shallow clones reference the source's data files without copying them.

**D** is wrong because deep clones are point-in-time snapshots, not live materialized views that refresh automatically.

[Clone a Delta table](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/clone.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst discovers that a batch job mistakenly overwrote a Delta table 3 hours ago (the table was at version 42 before the overwrite, which created version 43). The analyst wants to restore the table to version 42. Which SQL command performs this recovery?

options.text: SELECT * FROM sales.orders VERSION AS OF 42
RESTORE TABLE sales.orders TO VERSION AS OF 42
INSERT OVERWRITE sales.orders SELECT * FROM sales.orders VERSION AS OF 42
ROLLBACK sales.orders TO VERSION 42

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF <n>` is the Delta Lake command that reverts a table to a specific historical version. After execution, the table's current state reflects version 42 and a new version entry (e.g., version 44) is added to the transaction log recording the restore operation.

**A** is wrong because SELECT ... VERSION AS OF reads historical data but does not modify the table — it is a read-only Time Travel query.

**C** is wrong because while INSERT OVERWRITE with a Time Travel source would physically work, it is not the purpose-built restore command and does not record the operation cleanly as a restore in the table history.

**D** is wrong because ROLLBACK is not a valid Delta Lake or Spark SQL command — the correct command is RESTORE TABLE.

[RESTORE TABLE and Delta time travel](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/history.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to store documentation for a Delta table's columns directly in the table metadata so the descriptions appear in the Unity Catalog data explorer. Which SQL statement adds a description to a column named `order_status` on the `sales.orders` table?

options.text: ALTER TABLE sales.orders ALTER COLUMN order_status COMMENT 'Tracks the fulfillment state of each order: PENDING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED'
COMMENT ON COLUMN sales.orders.order_status IS 'Tracks the fulfillment state of each order: PENDING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED'
UPDATE COLUMN COMMENT sales.orders.order_status = 'Tracks the fulfillment state of each order'
ALTER TABLE sales.orders MODIFY COLUMN order_status SET COMMENT 'Tracks the fulfillment state of each order'

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... COMMENT '...'` is the valid Databricks SQL syntax for adding or updating a column-level comment. The description is stored in the Unity Catalog metastore and appears in the Data Explorer for all users with access to the table.

**B** is a plausible alternative that also works in Databricks SQL — `COMMENT ON COLUMN ... IS '...'` is the ANSI SQL standard form for column comments. However, the question asks which statement adds a column description, and **A** is the primary Databricks-documented form using the ALTER TABLE DDL.

**C** is wrong because `UPDATE COLUMN COMMENT` is not valid Databricks SQL syntax — there is no `UPDATE` DDL command for column comments.

**D** is wrong because `MODIFY COLUMN ... SET COMMENT` is MySQL/Hive syntax, not valid Databricks SQL. Databricks uses `ALTER COLUMN ... COMMENT` within an ALTER TABLE statement.

[Databricks Unity Catalog — manage table comments](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst is working with an `orders` table that has a `discount_pct` column containing some NULL values (meaning no discount applies). The analyst needs a query that returns 0 when `discount_pct` is NULL and the actual value otherwise. Additionally, the analyst wants to avoid division errors when computing `1 / discount_pct` — returning NULL when `discount_pct` is zero. Which combination of functions handles both cases in a single expression?

options.text: COALESCE(discount_pct, 0) for the NULL case; NULLIF(discount_pct, 0) for the division case — these are separate expressions for separate needs
NULLIF(COALESCE(discount_pct, 0), 0) — this replaces NULL with 0 and then makes 0 NULL, which would always return NULL
COALESCE(discount_pct, 0) for display and 1 / NULLIF(discount_pct, 0) for the reciprocal calculation — each NULLIF/COALESCE serves its specific purpose
IFNULL(discount_pct, 0) handles both cases: NULL values become 0 and 0 values are also safe to divide by

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the two requirements call for two different functions applied in different contexts. COALESCE(discount_pct, 0) is correct for displaying the discount (NULL becomes 0). For the reciprocal calculation, 1 / NULLIF(discount_pct, 0) is correct: NULLIF returns NULL when discount_pct is 0, making the division NULL/NULL safe — it avoids the division by zero error. Each function serves its specific use case.

**A** is also correct in that the functions are separate, but doesn't clearly describe how they're used in context.

**B** is wrong because nesting COALESCE then NULLIF would always return NULL for discount_pct that starts as NULL (NULL→0→NULL).

**D** is wrong because IFNULL does not provide any division-by-zero protection — dividing by 0 would still raise an error.

[COALESCE and NULLIF SQL functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/language-manual/sql-ref-functions-builtin.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst writes the following query to identify customers who have placed orders in 3 or more consecutive months. The analyst uses a window function approach:

```sql
SELECT customer_id, order_month,
  COUNT(*) OVER (
    PARTITION BY customer_id
    ORDER BY order_month
    ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS months_in_window
FROM (
  SELECT DISTINCT customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS order_month
  FROM orders
) monthly
QUALIFY months_in_window = 3;
```

What does `months_in_window = 3` in the QUALIFY clause indicate for a given row?

options.text: The customer has exactly 3 total orders in their history
The COUNT(*) counts only non-NULL months, so 3 means there are no NULL gaps in the window
The customer placed orders in exactly 3 different months across all time
The current month and the 2 preceding months in the window all contain at least one order for this customer

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because the inner query deduplicates to one row per customer per month. The COUNT(*) window function with ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW counts how many rows (distinct months) exist within the 3-row window (current month + up to 2 preceding months). When this count equals 3, it means the current month and the 2 immediately preceding months in the sorted sequence each had at least one order — indicating activity in 3 consecutive months (when months are sorted chronologically and the window spans exactly 3 rows).

**A** is wrong because the COUNT is within a sliding 3-row window per customer, not the total count across all history.

**C** is wrong because the window is bounded to 3 rows — a customer with orders in 10 months would have QUALIFY rows at multiple points.

**B** is wrong because COUNT(*) counts all rows in the window frame, not just non-NULLs — and the deduplicated source has no NULLs in order_month.

[QUALIFY clause and window functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to pad a product description column on the RIGHT with spaces to ensure it is always at least 30 characters wide for fixed-width report formatting. For example, 'Widget' (6 chars) should become 'Widget                        ' (30 chars). Which function accomplishes this?

options.text: LPAD(description, 30, ' ')
TRIM(description, 30)
RPAD(description, 30, ' ')
RIGHT(description, 30)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because RPAD(string, length, pad_string) pads the string on the RIGHT with the specified pad character until the total length reaches the target. RPAD('Widget', 30, ' ') produces 'Widget' followed by 24 spaces, for a total of 30 characters — suitable for fixed-width report alignment.

**A** is wrong because LPAD pads on the LEFT — it would add spaces before 'Widget', producing '                        Widget', which is not what is needed for this right-padding requirement.

**B** is wrong because TRIM removes characters and does not take a numeric length argument for padding.

**D** is wrong because RIGHT(string, n) returns the rightmost n characters of a string — it does not pad.

[RPAD and LPAD string functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst needs to build a report showing the running total of revenue by date, partitioned by region. The table `daily_sales(region, sale_date, revenue)` has one row per region per day. Select TWO expressions that correctly compute a running total of revenue within each region ordered by sale_date. (Select TWO)

options.text: SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_date)
SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)
SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` explicitly defines the window frame as starting at the first row of the partition and ending at the current row — the canonical running total definition.

**B** is correct because in Spark SQL, when an ORDER BY clause is present in the OVER clause without a ROWS/RANGE frame specification, the default frame is `RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` — which for most ordered numeric datasets also computes a running total. Both A and B produce the same running total in this scenario.

**C** is wrong because ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING computes a reverse-cumulative total (from the current row to the end), not a running total from the beginning.

**D** is wrong because ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING is a 3-row sliding window, not a cumulative sum from the beginning.

[Running total window functions](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to use a geospatial visualization to show sales totals by US state on a Databricks SQL dashboard. The query returns `state_code` (e.g., 'CA', 'TX') and `total_sales`. Which visualization type in Databricks SQL supports this geographic display?

options.text: Heatmap — configure state_code as the X-axis and total_sales as color intensity
Geospatial maps are not available in Databricks SQL — the data must be exported to an external tool like Tableau
Scatter plot — use longitude and latitude coordinates derived from state_code
Map visualization — select the choropleth map type and map state_code to the geographic field and total_sales to the color metric

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Databricks SQL includes a Map visualization type that supports choropleth (color-coded region) maps. When configured with a geographic identifier column (such as state codes, country codes, or FIPS codes) and a numeric metric column, the map visualization shades each geographic region by the metric value — perfect for displaying sales by state.

**A** is wrong because a heatmap in Databricks SQL is a 2D grid visualization for two categorical axes, not a geographic map.

**C** is wrong because a scatter plot uses numeric X and Y axes — while latitude/longitude scatter plots can approximate geographic layouts, they require explicit coordinate columns and do not render on a proper map background.

**B** is wrong because Map visualizations are available natively in Databricks SQL.

[Map visualization in Databricks SQL](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst is building a Databricks SQL dashboard that uses the `{{ @parameter }}` syntax (with the `@` prefix) instead of the standard `{{ parameter }}` syntax. What is the functional difference between these two parameter syntaxes in Databricks SQL?

options.text: {{ @parameter }} is used for dashboard-level parameters that are shared across all queries; {{ parameter }} is used for query-level parameters local to a single query
{{ @parameter }} inserts the parameter value without surrounding quotes (useful for column names or SQL keywords); {{ parameter }} inserts the value as a quoted string literal
{{ @parameter }} is the legacy syntax from older versions; {{ parameter }} is the current recommended syntax — they are functionally equivalent in modern Databricks SQL
{{ @parameter }} allows multi-value parameters for IN clauses; {{ parameter }} only supports single-value substitution

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because in Databricks SQL parameterized queries, the `{{ parameter }}` syntax (without @) substitutes the value as a quoted string literal — suitable for filter values in WHERE clauses. The `{{ @parameter }}` syntax (with @) substitutes the value without adding surrounding quotes — this is the 'raw' or 'unquoted' substitution mode, used when the parameter value represents an identifier (like a column name, table name, or SQL expression) that should not be wrapped in quotes.

**A** is wrong because both syntaxes can be used at the dashboard level for shared parameters — the difference is quoting behavior, not scope.

**C** is wrong because these are distinct functional forms, not legacy vs current syntax.

**D** is wrong because multi-value parameter support is controlled by the parameter configuration (Allow multiple values setting), not by the @ symbol in the substitution syntax.

[Parameterized query syntax — @ prefix](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A marketing analyst wants to visualize a customer acquisition funnel showing the drop-off at each stage: Awareness (10,000), Interest (4,500), Consideration (2,100), Intent (800), Purchase (300). The analyst wants a chart type that shows the sequential narrowing of the customer base through these stages. Which chart type is most appropriate?

options.text: Stacked bar chart with one bar per funnel stage
Funnel chart showing the sequential narrowing through each stage
Line chart with stages on the X-axis and count on the Y-axis
Pie chart with 5 slices showing the share of customers at each stage

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a Funnel chart is specifically designed to visualize a sequential process where the population narrows at each stage — it visually conveys both the absolute count at each stage and the proportional drop-off between stages, making the conversion bottlenecks immediately apparent. Funnel charts are available in Databricks SQL.

**A** is wrong because stacked bars are better for comparing parts of a whole across multiple categories — they do not naturally convey the sequential narrowing and drop-off of a funnel.

**C** is wrong because a line chart implies a continuous trend over time or a smooth relationship between values — the discrete stages of a marketing funnel are not well represented by a connected line.

**D** is wrong because a pie chart shows proportional composition of a whole at one point in time — it does not convey the sequential funnel structure or the drop-off narrative.

[Funnel chart visualization](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/user/dashboards/index.html)

questions.stem:
A data analyst creates the following parameterized query on a Databricks SQL dashboard:

```sql
SELECT * FROM sales
WHERE region IN ({{ regions }})
```

The `regions` parameter is configured as a Query Dropdown with 'Allow multiple values' enabled. A user selects 'East' and 'West' from the dropdown. What SQL is effectively executed?

options.text: SELECT * FROM sales WHERE region IN ('East,West')
SELECT * FROM sales WHERE region IN ('East', 'West')
SELECT * FROM sales WHERE region IN (East, West)
SELECT * FROM sales WHERE region IN ({{ 'East', 'West' }})

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because when a Query Dropdown parameter with 'Allow multiple values' is used inside an IN() clause, Databricks SQL expands the selected values into a properly quoted, comma-separated list. Selecting 'East' and 'West' results in the parameter being expanded to `'East', 'West'` — producing `WHERE region IN ('East', 'West')`.

**A** is wrong because the two values are not concatenated into a single string — they are expanded as separate quoted values.

**C** is wrong because the values are quoted strings, not bare identifiers.

**D** is wrong because the final executed SQL uses the expanded values directly, not the template syntax.

[Multi-value dropdown parameters](https://docs.databricks.com/sql/index.html)

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that a query on a large Delta table scans many more files than expected, even though the table has been OPTIMIZED with ZORDER BY on the filter column. The analyst checks the table statistics and finds they are out of date. Which SQL command refreshes column-level statistics so the query optimizer can accurately use data skipping?

options.text: REFRESH TABLE sales.orders
OPTIMIZE sales.orders ZORDER BY (customer_id)
ANALYZE TABLE sales.orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS customer_id, order_date
DESCRIBE DETAIL sales.orders

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS ...` collects per-column statistics (min, max, distinct count, null count, average column length) for the specified columns and stores them in the metastore. The Spark query optimizer uses these statistics for cardinality estimates and file pruning decisions — keeping them current allows the optimizer to correctly skip files that cannot contain matching rows.

**A** is wrong because REFRESH TABLE synchronizes the metastore metadata (file list) with the physical storage — it does not compute column-level cardinality or distribution statistics.

**B** is wrong because OPTIMIZE compacts files and applies Z-Ordering for co-location — it does not generate column-level statistics for the optimizer.

**D** is wrong because DESCRIBE DETAIL reads existing table metadata; it does not compute or update statistics.

[ANALYZE TABLE and column statistics](https://docs.databricks.com/delta/optimizations/file-mgmt.html)
